content: Scripture affirms every child as God's handiwork (Psalm 139:13 16). The child is not punished for the father's sin (Ezekiel 18:20). Christ welcomed little ones without distinction (Mark 10:14). Justice prosecutes the rapist; mercy protects the child. Abortion compounds evil with another killing.

title: What legal strategies end abortion without exceptions?
excerpt: Pass abolition bills that establish equal protection from conception, criminalize all intentional killing of preborn children, and remove immunity that treats mothers or abortionists differently. Reject regulatory schemes that permit some murders (gestational limits, exceptions). Magistrates must fear God, not…
content: Pass abolition bills that establish equal protection from conception, criminalize all intentional killing of preborn children, and remove immunity that treats mothers or abortionists differently. Reject regulatory schemes that permit some murders (gestational limits, exceptions). Magistrates must fear God, not man (2 Samuel 23:3), and uphold justice consistently.

title: How can Christians discern pro-life legislation that compromises justice?
excerpt: Ask: Does this legislation protect every child from conception without exception? Does the bill outlaw all abortion as murder and require equal justice (Proverbs 24:11–12; Romans 13:3–4)? Does the bill explicitly state when 'abortion' is legal, or how to do…
content: Ask: Does this legislation protect every child from conception without exception? Does the bill outlaw all abortion as murder and require equal justice (Proverbs 24:11–12; Romans 13:3–4)? Does the bill explicitly state when 'abortion' is legal, or how to do it legally? Does it refer to it as a medical procedure? Bills that regulate murder (gestational limits, exceptions, immunity for mothers who self abort) compromise God’s law. Support abolition bills that establish equal protection, and support these bills ONLY. Support of pro life bills only serves to delay justice and is an abomination to our Great God.

title: What steps should fathers take when mothers consider abortion?
excerpt: Repent of passivity; lovingly plead for the child's life; provide tangible support and protection; involve church shepherds; seek just legal recourse if needed. Fathers must reflect Christ's sacrificial headship (Ephesians 5:25) and obey Proverbs 24:11 by intervening to rescue. Pray,…
content: Repent of passivity; lovingly plead for the child's life; provide tangible support and protection; involve church shepherds; seek just legal recourse if needed. Fathers must reflect Christ's sacrificial headship (Ephesians 5:25) and obey Proverbs 24:11 by intervening to rescue. Pray, stand firm, and offer ongoing care for mother and baby.

title: Does a detectable heartbeat prove personhood scientifically and biblically?
excerpt: Heartbeat detection simply reveals what God made at conception: a human person (Psalm 139:13–16). Personhood does not begin with a detectable pulse; life begins when God knits the child together at conception. Heartbeat laws only serve to create no protection…
content: Heartbeat detection simply reveals what God made at conception: a human person (Psalm 139:13–16). Personhood does not begin with a detectable pulse; life begins when God knits the child together at conception. Heartbeat laws only serve to create no protection for people before a heartbeat, implying that it's OK to murder children prior to detection. Justice must guard the preborn from conception, without exceptions. Additionally, from a biblical standpoint, abortion is an act of murder. So the expect that the very people who are intent upon murdering their own child, or the people who are being paid to murder someone's child would not lie while pretending to look for a heartbeat is naïve at best, and sinister at worst.

title: How do we answer claims that abortion is healthcare?
excerpt: We answer that claim by going back to the truth about what abortion is . Scripture and reason both reveal that abortion is the intentional killing of an innocent human being . No act that deliberately takes innocent human life…
content: We answer that claim by going back to the truth about what abortion is . Scripture and reason both reveal that abortion is the intentional killing of an innocent human being . No act that deliberately takes innocent human life can rightly be called “healthcare.” Healthcare, properly defined, is the practice of preserving and restoring health . It aims to protect life, heal the sick, and care for the weak. Abortion, by contrast, ends the life of one human being and often harms the body and soul of another. To call it “healthcare” is to corrupt language in order to justify evil—much like calling slavery “property management” or genocide “population control.” It is a lie that disguises murder as mercy (Isaiah 5:20). From a biblical standpoint: God alone has the authority to give and take life (Deuteronomy 32:39). He commands, “You shall not murder” (Exodus 20:13). The civil magistrate’s duty is to protect the innocent, not authorize their destruction (Romans 13:3–4). Thus, abortion is not medicine; it is rebellion against the Creator and injustice against one’s smallest neighbor. Real healthcare would treat both the mother and the child as patients worthy of protection and care. It would seek to preserve both lives, not pit them against each other. As abolitionists, we must also expose how the “healthcare” claim functions strategically—it reframes moral evil as medical necessity to numb consciences and legalize murder. Christians must reject that framing entirely, not debate it on its own terms. The issue is not about access to medical services; it is about obedience to God’s law and justice for the innocent .

title: If infants that die go to Heaven, why is abortion a problem?
excerpt: That question goes right to the heart of the matter, and it’s one that deserves a clear and biblical answer. First, even if every aborted child were immediately received into the presence of Christ—which many Reformed Baptists and other Protestants…
content: That question goes right to the heart of the matter, and it’s one that deserves a clear and biblical answer. First, even if every aborted child were immediately received into the presence of Christ—which many Reformed Baptists and other Protestants believe is indeed the case—that would not make abortion any less evil. Scripture is clear that we are never permitted to do evil that good may come (Romans 3:8). The fact that God can redeem evil actions and bring good from them does not justify committing those actions. God’s sovereignty never excuses man’s sin. Abortion is murder—the intentional killing of an innocent human being. Each child in the womb is a living soul made by God (Psalm 139:13–16; Jeremiah 1:5). The moral question, therefore, is not what happens to the baby afterward , but what man has done before God in shedding innocent blood. We might also ask: would it be righteous for us to kill all infants, or all Christians, because we believe they would go to Heaven? Of course not. That would be rebellion against God’s law. Abortion is not a mercy; it is the unjust destruction of a neighbor whom we are commanded to love (Matthew 22:39). In addition, God uses even the short lives of those little ones to testify to His justice and grace. Their deaths are not meaningless, but their murderers will be held accountable unless they repent and trust in Christ. God commands rulers to protect innocent life, not to destroy it (Romans 13:3–4). The abolitionist stance is simply obedience to that command—calling both individuals and nations to repent immediately and abolish abortion entirely , not regulate or excuse it.

title: When does human life begin?
excerpt: Biblical Answer: In the book of Job, the patriarch exclaims that he was a living being "from my mother's womb" (Job 3:11). Job says, "why did I not die at birth?", indicating he was alive in the womb before he…
content: Biblical Answer: In the book of Job, the patriarch exclaims that he was a living being "from my mother's womb" (Job 3:11). Job says, "why did I not die at birth?", indicating he was alive in the womb before he was born. Additionally, in the Gospel of Luke, we read that when Mary visited Elizabeth, the unborn John the Baptist leaped in her womb upon hearing Mary's greeting (Luke 1:41). These passages indicate that life and personhood are present even while in the womb. Scientific Answer: According to the position paper by the American College of Pediatricians titled <a href="https://acpeds.org/when human life begins/" target=" blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" When Human Life Begins </a , "research in embryology and related biological sciences overwhelmingly indicates that human life begins at conception (fertilization)." At fertilization the zygote is a living, genetically distinct human organism who will grow and develop through every subsequent stage of life.

title: If abortion can be forgiven, why not do it and just ask for forgiveness after?
excerpt: This question highlights a key distinction in how many people think about sin and forgiveness. Although God's grace is sufficient to forgive any sin His people commit or have committed Scripture repeatedly warns us to never presume upon that grace…
content: This question highlights a key distinction in how many people think about sin and forgiveness. Although God's grace is sufficient to forgive any sin His people commit or have committed Scripture repeatedly warns us to never presume upon that grace (Romans 6:1 2). We should not sin with the expectation of forgiveness (Romans 3:8). If you find yourself thinking that way, it is a sign you have not yet bowed your knee to Christ as Lord; you likely do not have eternal life abiding in you (1 John 2:3 4)! In fact, Scripture warns that presumption and arrogance amount to idolatry (1 Samuel 15:22 23). When you assume God will forgive a sin you knowingly engage in, you are believing in a false god who cannot save you (Exodus 20:3; Galatians 6:7 8). The Bible commands us to flee sinful passions and trust that what God has said is true: righteous choices are always what is best for ourselves and others (Titus 2:11 12; Psalm 19:7 11).

title: How should church discipline address members who advocate for abortion?
excerpt: Christ has given the keys to the church to guard the gospel and the flock (Matthew 18; 1 Corinthians 5). If a member advocates for abortion, elders must warn, instruct, and call to repentance. Persisting in promoting murder hardens the…
content: Christ has given the keys to the church to guard the gospel and the flock (Matthew 18; 1 Corinthians 5). If a member advocates for abortion, elders must warn, instruct, and call to repentance. Persisting in promoting murder hardens the conscience and dishonors Christ; loving discipline, up to excommunication if unrepentant, aims to restore the sinner and protect the assembly.

title: How can Christian families plan to stand with mothers in unplanned pregnancies?
excerpt: Families can choose hospitality ahead of time: set aside budget, bedrooms, meals, and time to help mothers in crisis (Galatians 6:2). Coordinate with your elders so care is shepherded, not solo. Practice saying, "We will stand with you and your…
content: Families can choose hospitality ahead of time: set aside budget, bedrooms, meals, and time to help mothers in crisis (Galatians 6:2). Coordinate with your elders so care is shepherded, not solo. Practice saying, "We will stand with you and your child," then back it with childcare, transportation, and advocacy. Trust the Lord who provides when His people open their homes (1 Peter 4:9).

title: How should Christians engage medical providers who perform abortions?
excerpt: Approach physicians with truth and sobriety: abortion is the shedding of innocent blood (Exodus 20:13; Proverbs 6:17). Appeal to their conscience and professional oath to heal, not kill. Offer the gospel of Christ who forgives every repentant sinner (1 John…
content: Approach physicians with truth and sobriety: abortion is the shedding of innocent blood (Exodus 20:13; Proverbs 6:17). Appeal to their conscience and professional oath to heal, not kill. Offer the gospel of Christ who forgives every repentant sinner (1 John 1:9), and urge them to cease, confess, and make restitution. Partner with ethical providers to show there is a faithful way to practice medicine.

title: What role do Psalm 82 and Micah 6:8 play in shaping abolition?
excerpt: Psalm 82 rebukes rulers who fail to rescue the weak; Micah 6:8 commands us to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with God. Together they frame abolition: defend the fatherless, refuse partiality, and act under God’s gaze. Abortion is…
content: Psalm 82 rebukes rulers who fail to rescue the weak; Micah 6:8 commands us to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with God. Together they frame abolition: defend the fatherless, refuse partiality, and act under God’s gaze. Abortion is legalized oppression; these texts demand we oppose it with justice rooted in worship.

title: How can Christians discern ethical responses to insurance plans that cover abortion?
excerpt: If an insurance plan funds abortion, believers must not participate in the shedding of innocent blood (Romans 1:32). Seek alternatives: opt out riders, employer negotiations, health share ministries, or conscience accommodations. If constrained, protest in writing, pursue change, and consider…
content: If an insurance plan funds abortion, believers must not participate in the shedding of innocent blood (Romans 1:32). Seek alternatives: opt out riders, employer negotiations, health share ministries, or conscience accommodations. If constrained, protest in writing, pursue change, and consider costly obedience for a clear conscience (Acts 5:29). Trust the Lord to provide as you refuse to finance murder.

title: Should Christians pursue embryo adoption from IVF clinics?
excerpt: Embryos are image bearers, not surplus property (Psalm 139:13 16). Rescuing frozen children can be an act of mercy, yet the IVF system that created them is unjust. Couples must proceed with sober conscience, refusing to participate in further creation…
content: Embryos are image bearers, not surplus property (Psalm 139:13 16). Rescuing frozen children can be an act of mercy, yet the IVF system that created them is unjust. Couples must proceed with sober conscience, refusing to participate in further creation or destruction of embryos, and seek pastoral wisdom. Whatever is not from faith is sin (Romans 14:23).

title: How can churches disciple youth for abolition?
excerpt: Disciple teenagers with Scripture’s view of life, sin, and the cross. Walk through Proverbs 24:11 12, Psalm 139, and Luke 1 so they see God’s heart. Train them in prayer, mercy, and courage, paired with practical service alongside mature believers.…
content: Disciple teenagers with Scripture’s view of life, sin, and the cross. Walk through Proverbs 24:11 12, Psalm 139, and Luke 1 so they see God’s heart. Train them in prayer, mercy, and courage, paired with practical service alongside mature believers. Keep the gospel central so zeal flows from love, not mere outrage.

title: How does the Great Commission shape abolition work?
excerpt: Jesus commands us to make disciples, teaching them to obey all He commanded (Matthew 28:18 20). That includes rescuing the weak, refusing murder, and loving neighbors in the womb. Abolition flows from disciple making: evangelize, baptize, and train believers to…
content: Jesus commands us to make disciples, teaching them to obey all He commanded (Matthew 28:18 20). That includes rescuing the weak, refusing murder, and loving neighbors in the womb. Abolition flows from disciple making: evangelize, baptize, and train believers to keep Christ’s law in public and private, confident He is with us to the end.

title: How should Christians respond when churches refuse to address abortion?
excerpt: Appeal humbly to leaders with Scripture (Proverbs 24:11 12; Psalm 82). If they refuse to name abortion as murder and shepherd the flock, continue to exhort with patience, but seek faithful oversight where the whole counsel of God is preached…
content: Appeal humbly to leaders with Scripture (Proverbs 24:11 12; Psalm 82). If they refuse to name abortion as murder and shepherd the flock, continue to exhort with patience, but seek faithful oversight where the whole counsel of God is preached (Acts 20:27). Silence in the face of bloodshed is negligence; love requires truthful, pastoral action.

title: What guidance does Galatians 6 give for bearing the burdens of pregnant mothers?
excerpt: Galatians 6 calls us to bear one another’s burdens and fulfill the law of Christ. For pregnant mothers, this means practical aid, patient listening, and restoring the fainthearted in gentleness. We sow to the Spirit when we invest time, money,…
content: Galatians 6 calls us to bear one another’s burdens and fulfill the law of Christ. For pregnant mothers, this means practical aid, patient listening, and restoring the fainthearted in gentleness. We sow to the Spirit when we invest time, money, and prayer into their good, trusting God’s harvest in due season.

title: How can small groups support sidewalk ministry outside abortion centers?
excerpt: Small groups can pray, fast, and rotate teams to be present where children are endangered. Provide literature, offer help to mothers, and connect them immediately to church support. Follow up faithfully; the goal is not protests but rescue and discipleship.…
content: Small groups can pray, fast, and rotate teams to be present where children are endangered. Provide literature, offer help to mothers, and connect them immediately to church support. Follow up faithfully; the goal is not protests but rescue and discipleship. Let your speech be gracious and truthful, seasoned with the gospel (Colossians 4:6).

title: How do we answer claims that abolition is too harsh?
excerpt: God forbids partiality in justice (Deuteronomy 1:17). Abolition is not harsh; it is consistent love for every image bearer and equal weights and measures. Softening justice for the preborn denies their humanity. We pair truthful law with rich mercy for…
content: God forbids partiality in justice (Deuteronomy 1:17). Abolition is not harsh; it is consistent love for every image bearer and equal weights and measures. Softening justice for the preborn denies their humanity. We pair truthful law with rich mercy for mothers and fathers, holding out Christ who saves sinners and upholds righteousness.

title: What is biblical equal protection for the preborn?
excerpt: Equal protection means the same laws against murder apply to every human from conception without exception (Genesis 9:6). No immunity for age, location, or stage; no partiality for the powerful. Civil rulers are God’s servants to punish evil (Romans 13:4).…
content: Equal protection means the same laws against murder apply to every human from conception without exception (Genesis 9:6). No immunity for age, location, or stage; no partiality for the powerful. Civil rulers are God’s servants to punish evil (Romans 13:4). Anything less tolerates injustice; true love demands equal justice and equal mercy.

title: How should Christians view the dignity of fetal remains after abortion?
excerpt: Even after death, human remains deserve dignity because they belonged to an image bearer (Deuteronomy 21:22 23). Aborted children should not be treated as medical waste or research material. Christians should call for respectful handling, lament the injustice, and labor…
content: Even after death, human remains deserve dignity because they belonged to an image bearer (Deuteronomy 21:22 23). Aborted children should not be treated as medical waste or research material. Christians should call for respectful handling, lament the injustice, and labor to end the bloodshed that creates this sorrow.

title: How can churches prepare legal help for mothers pressured to abort?
excerpt: Churches can identify trusted attorneys, establish benevolence funds, and create rapid response teams to stand with mothers facing coercion. Teach members their rights, document threats, and intervene early. Proverbs 31:8 9 calls us to speak up for those being crushed,…
content: Churches can identify trusted attorneys, establish benevolence funds, and create rapid response teams to stand with mothers facing coercion. Teach members their rights, document threats, and intervene early. Proverbs 31:8 9 calls us to speak up for those being crushed, the voiceless; legal help is one way love protects both mother and child. Although women are guilty of murder in God's eyes when they kill their children, many women feel very conflicted as their conscience accuses them during the decision making process. It is during this time that firm proclamation of the evil of abortion and tangible help being offered should display the light of Christ to those in need of both these things. Churches ought to start and support actual gospel proclaiming pregnancy resource centers where women can receive good counsel and the support they need as mothers.

title: What pastoral care do men need after coercing abortion?
excerpt: Men who coerced abortion need conviction, confession, and the cleansing blood of Christ (1 John 1:9). Pastors should call them to repentance, require restitution where possible, and shepherd them into accountable community. Grace trains us to renounce ungodliness (Titus 2:11…
content: Men who coerced abortion need conviction, confession, and the cleansing blood of Christ (1 John 1:9). Pastors should call them to repentance, require restitution where possible, and shepherd them into accountable community. Grace trains us to renounce ungodliness (Titus 2:11 12); disciplined love aims for restored, holy manhood.

title: How should Christians view genetic testing when Down syndrome is suspected?
excerpt: Genetic screenings must not become a pretext for murder. Every child, including those with Down syndrome, bears God’s image (Genesis 1:27). Testing may inform care, but it must never guide toward abortion. Christians should reject selective killing, offer robust support…
content: Genetic screenings must not become a pretext for murder. Every child, including those with Down syndrome, bears God’s image (Genesis 1:27). Testing may inform care, but it must never guide toward abortion. Christians should reject selective killing, offer robust support to families, and celebrate the dignity of every child.

title: How can churches serve college students facing pregnancy?
excerpt: Meet students with compassion, not shame. Provide housing, transportation, childcare help, and academic advocacy. Connect them with older women for discipleship (Titus 2), and with physicians who refuse abortion. The church’s welcome should show that Christ cares for mother and…
content: Meet students with compassion, not shame. Provide housing, transportation, childcare help, and academic advocacy. Connect them with older women for discipleship (Titus 2), and with physicians who refuse abortion. The church’s welcome should show that Christ cares for mother and child, even when campus culture pressures abandonment.

title: What liturgical practices help churches lament abortion?
excerpt: Corporate lament teaches us to hate what God hates and hope in His mercy. Use Psalms of lament, pastoral prayers that name the sin of abortion, and assurance of pardon in Christ. Periodic days of fasting and prayer can cultivate…
content: Corporate lament teaches us to hate what God hates and hope in His mercy. Use Psalms of lament, pastoral prayers that name the sin of abortion, and assurance of pardon in Christ. Periodic days of fasting and prayer can cultivate earnestness without replacing obedience. Lament should move us to rescue and to rest in the gospel.

title: How does the incarnation inform our view of the unborn?
excerpt: The eternal Son took on flesh and was first known as a child in the womb (Luke 1). The incarnation dignifies life from conception; God dwelt among us beginning in hiddenness. To assault the womb is to despise the pattern…
content: The eternal Son took on flesh and was first known as a child in the womb (Luke 1). The incarnation dignifies life from conception; God dwelt among us beginning in hiddenness. To assault the womb is to despise the pattern God honored. Also see Luke 1:41,44

title: How can Christian businesses stand against abortion mandates?
excerpt: Christian employers must obey God rather than men (Acts 5:29). Challenge abortion mandates through legal channels, seek exemptions, and refuse to pay for the shedding of innocent blood. Care well for employees by offering alternatives that uphold life. Courageous obedience…
content: Christian employers must obey God rather than men (Acts 5:29). Challenge abortion mandates through legal channels, seek exemptions, and refuse to pay for the shedding of innocent blood. Care well for employees by offering alternatives that uphold life. Courageous obedience may be costly, but Christ is Lord over every ledger and policy.

title: What should elders teach men about sexual sin and abortion demand?
excerpt: Elders must confront lust, pornography, and fornication that fuel abortion demand. Call men to purity, covenant faithfulness, and fatherly courage (Ephesians 5:25; 1 Thessalonians 4:3 4). Teach that abandoning mother and child is cowardice; Christ’s love sets men free to…
content: Elders must confront lust, pornography, and fornication that fuel abortion demand. Call men to purity, covenant faithfulness, and fatherly courage (Ephesians 5:25; 1 Thessalonians 4:3 4). Teach that abandoning mother and child is cowardice; Christ’s love sets men free to lead, provide, and protect.

title: How can churches structure budgets to fund mercy for mothers?
excerpt: Build mercy for mothers into the church budget, not as an afterthought. Fund housing help, childcare, medical costs, and legal aid as needed. Deacons can steward these resources (Acts 6) while pastors shepherd souls. Sacrificial generosity displays the gospel to…
content: Build mercy for mothers into the church budget, not as an afterthought. Fund housing help, childcare, medical costs, and legal aid as needed. Deacons can steward these resources (Acts 6) while pastors shepherd souls. Sacrificial generosity displays the gospel to parents tempted by fear.

title: What role do prayer and fasting play in abolition?
excerpt: Prayer and fasting humble us before God and align us with His purposes (Isaiah 58). They are not substitutes for obedience but fuel for faithfulness. As we seek His face, we gain courage to rescue and persevere. The battle is…
content: Prayer and fasting humble us before God and align us with His purposes (Isaiah 58). They are not substitutes for obedience but fuel for faithfulness. As we seek His face, we gain courage to rescue and persevere. The battle is spiritual and demands dependence on Christ’s power, not human strength.

title: How should Christians interpret Exodus 21 in the abortion debate?
excerpt: Exodus 21:22 25 treats harm to the unborn with the same lex talionis standard applied to all persons. If there is injury, the penalty is life for life, eye for eye. The passage affirms personhood in the womb and God’s…
content: Exodus 21:22 25 treats harm to the unborn with the same lex talionis standard applied to all persons. If there is injury, the penalty is life for life, eye for eye. The passage affirms personhood in the womb and God’s demand for equal justice. It undercuts any claim that Scripture permits lesser protections for the preborn.

title: Should Christians prioritize federal abolition or state-level strategies?
excerpt: Both state and federal spheres are accountable to God. Pursue abolition wherever jurisdiction allows: press your state to enact equal protection now, and urge federal authorities to uphold justice nationally. Reject delay framed as strategy; partiality is sin. We labor…
content: Both state and federal spheres are accountable to God. Pursue abolition wherever jurisdiction allows: press your state to enact equal protection now, and urge federal authorities to uphold justice nationally. Reject delay framed as strategy; partiality is sin. We labor on all fronts, trusting Christ to honor faithfulness more than political calculus.

title: How can Christians guard against burnout in abolition ministry?
excerpt: Burnout comes when we carry the load apart from Christ. Rest in the finished work of Jesus, keep the Sabbath rhythm, share the load with the body, and remember that results belong to God (1 Corinthians 3:6 7). Take joy…
content: Burnout comes when we carry the load apart from Christ. Rest in the finished work of Jesus, keep the Sabbath rhythm, share the load with the body, and remember that results belong to God (1 Corinthians 3:6 7). Take joy in small obediences; do not confuse urgency with unbelieving frenzy. The Lord is our strength.

title: What steps should churches take when a member procures an abortion?
excerpt: Churches must respond with both truth and restoration. Pursue Matthew 18: private confrontation, then witnesses, then the church if unrepentant. Hold out Christ’s full pardon to the penitent and walk with them through grief and restitution. Discipline aims at recovery,…
content: Churches must respond with both truth and restoration. Pursue Matthew 18: private confrontation, then witnesses, then the church if unrepentant. Hold out Christ’s full pardon to the penitent and walk with them through grief and restitution. Discipline aims at recovery, not shame, and protects the flock from treating murder lightly.

title: How can Christians respond to family pressure to abort?
excerpt: Honor parents, but fear God first (Acts 5:29). When family urges abortion, respond with gentle firmness: the child is God’s gift and not ours to kill. Seek church support, invite family to hear the gospel, and set boundaries against coercion.…
content: Honor parents, but fear God first (Acts 5:29). When family urges abortion, respond with gentle firmness: the child is God’s gift and not ours to kill. Seek church support, invite family to hear the gospel, and set boundaries against coercion. Love them, but obey Christ above all. If you are in a situation where people threaten to take away your home or support unless you murder your child, you must find a way to protect yourself and your child from these people.

title: How does Jesus welcoming children shape abolition?
excerpt: Jesus received little children and rebuked those who hindered them (Mark 10:13 16). If the King welcomes the smallest, His people must too. Abolition is simply living out Christ’s welcome: protecting, blessing, and making room for every child, because the…
content: Jesus received little children and rebuked those who hindered them (Mark 10:13 16). If the King welcomes the smallest, His people must too. Abolition is simply living out Christ’s welcome: protecting, blessing, and making room for every child, because the kingdom belongs to such as these.

title: What hymns and Scriptures encourage mothers choosing life?
excerpt: Read and pray Psalm 23, Psalm 139, and Romans 8 to fix hope on the Lord’s nearness. Sing hymns like “Great Is Thy Faithfulness” and “All Must Be Well” to steady fearful hearts. Scripture and song remind mothers that Christ…
content: Read and pray Psalm 23, Psalm 139, and Romans 8 to fix hope on the Lord’s nearness. Sing hymns like “Great Is Thy Faithfulness” and “All Must Be Well” to steady fearful hearts. Scripture and song remind mothers that Christ is with them and that the church will stand beside them.

title: How can Christians engage local media with the abolition message?
excerpt: Speak plainly and respectfully about abortion as the unjust taking of human life. Offer stories of mercy ministry that back your words. Anchor claims in Scripture and basic science, and call civil leaders to equal protection. Refuse euphemisms; let your…
content: Speak plainly and respectfully about abortion as the unjust taking of human life. Offer stories of mercy ministry that back your words. Anchor claims in Scripture and basic science, and call civil leaders to equal protection. Refuse euphemisms; let your light shine before others, pointing to Christ’s authority (Matthew 5:16).

title: Should Christians use graphic imagery in abolition outreach?
excerpt: Graphic images can awaken the conscience to abortion’s cruelty, but they can also harden or retraumatize. Use them, if at all, with pastoral care, lawful conduct, and readiness to offer the gospel and tangible help. Whatever you do, let love…
content: Graphic images can awaken the conscience to abortion’s cruelty, but they can also harden or retraumatize. Use them, if at all, with pastoral care, lawful conduct, and readiness to offer the gospel and tangible help. Whatever you do, let love for neighbor and reverence for God govern your methods (1 Corinthians 16:14).

title: How can rural churches address abortion pill mailing into their communities?
excerpt: Rural areas are not immune to pill trafficking. Teach the saints to recognize and resist it, partner with magistrates to enforce just laws, and offer help to mothers before and after birth. Use local networks—churches, clinics, schools—to spread truth and…
content: Rural areas are not immune to pill trafficking. Teach the saints to recognize and resist it, partner with magistrates to enforce just laws, and offer help to mothers before and after birth. Use local networks—churches, clinics, schools—to spread truth and provide support faster than pills can arrive.

title: What does justice require for abortionists who repent?
excerpt: God’s justice requires truthful reckoning: those who shed innocent blood are liable to punishment (Genesis 9:6; Romans 13:4). If an abortionist repents, the church extends gospel forgiveness and discipleship, yet civil authorities must still do their duty. Mercy in Christ…
content: God’s justice requires truthful reckoning: those who shed innocent blood are liable to punishment (Genesis 9:6; Romans 13:4). If an abortionist repents, the church extends gospel forgiveness and discipleship, yet civil authorities must still do their duty. Mercy in Christ does not erase earthly justice; both can honor God’s holiness.

title: How should Christians think about profit-based surrogacy?
excerpt: Profit driven surrogacy treats wombs and children as commodities. Scripture teaches that children are a heritage from the Lord (Psalm 127:3), not products to buy or rent. Christians should reject arrangements that fracture motherhood for money and instead pursue ethical…
content: Profit driven surrogacy treats wombs and children as commodities. Scripture teaches that children are a heritage from the Lord (Psalm 127:3), not products to buy or rent. Christians should reject arrangements that fracture motherhood for money and instead pursue ethical care and adoption that honors every party as an image bearer.

title: What role should abolition play in church missions strategy?
excerpt: Missions aims to make disciples who obey Jesus everywhere (Matthew 28:18 20). That includes teaching nations to cherish life and reject murder. As churches plant and send, they should equip workers to confront abortion with the gospel, mercy, and just…
content: Missions aims to make disciples who obey Jesus everywhere (Matthew 28:18 20). That includes teaching nations to cherish life and reject murder. As churches plant and send, they should equip workers to confront abortion with the gospel, mercy, and just teaching, so local churches abroad become voices for the voiceless.

title: How can churches disciple new believers rescued from abortion culture?
excerpt: New believers need milk and meat: clear teaching on grace, repentance, and the lordship of Christ. Address past sins, including abortion, with gospel hope (Isaiah 1:18) and practical accountability. Integrate them into ordinary means of grace, mentoring, and service so…
content: New believers need milk and meat: clear teaching on grace, repentance, and the lordship of Christ. Address past sins, including abortion, with gospel hope (Isaiah 1:18) and practical accountability. Integrate them into ordinary means of grace, mentoring, and service so they grow in holiness and love for life.

title: What can Christians learn from Wilberforce and abolition history?
excerpt: Wilberforce modeled patient, principled persistence against entrenched evil. He paired legislative labor with mercy to the oppressed and unwavering conviction rooted in the gospel. Christians today should imitate his refusal to compromise, his coalition building, and his confidence that Christ’s…
content: Wilberforce modeled patient, principled persistence against entrenched evil. He paired legislative labor with mercy to the oppressed and unwavering conviction rooted in the gospel. Christians today should imitate his refusal to compromise, his coalition building, and his confidence that Christ’s kingdom advances through truth and love.

title: How can churches measure faithful engagement against abortion?
excerpt: Measure faithfulness, not publicity: Are we preaching clearly? Are members serving mothers and fathers? Are we advocating for equal justice? Are we praying and fasting? Track tangible care (housing, meals, legal help), discipleship, and public witness. Numbers matter less than…
content: Measure faithfulness, not publicity: Are we preaching clearly? Are members serving mothers and fathers? Are we advocating for equal justice? Are we praying and fasting? Track tangible care (housing, meals, legal help), discipleship, and public witness. Numbers matter less than steadfast obedience from a pure heart.

title: What is the biblical case against abortion exceptions for rape or incest?
excerpt: Scripture forbids punishing children for their fathers' sins (Ezekiel 18:20). Each child is knit by God and belongs to Him (Psalm 139:13 16). Justice demands we prosecute the rapist and protect the baby. Exceptions make innocence a matter of circumstance;…
content: Scripture forbids punishing children for their fathers' sins (Ezekiel 18:20). Each child is knit by God and belongs to Him (Psalm 139:13 16). Justice demands we prosecute the rapist and protect the baby. Exceptions make innocence a matter of circumstance; God calls all shedding of innocent blood evil.

title: How can Christians correct pro-life arguments that deny equal justice?
excerpt: When pro life arguments excuse mothers or permit regulated killing, expose the partiality (Proverbs 24:11 12). Point to God’s equal standards and the command of God in the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:6). Call for consistency: true love seeks both mercy…
content: When pro life arguments excuse mothers or permit regulated killing, expose the partiality (Proverbs 24:11 12). Point to God’s equal standards and the command of God in the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:6). Call for consistency: true love seeks both mercy and justice, not immunity that leaves some lives unprotected.

title: How can churches train women to mentor young mothers?
excerpt: Older women should train younger women to love their children and be self controlled (Titus 2:3 5). Build mentoring pairs, offer classes on newborn care, budgeting, and prayer, and include the gospel at every step. Mentorship turns doctrine into embodied…
content: Older women should train younger women to love their children and be self controlled (Titus 2:3 5). Build mentoring pairs, offer classes on newborn care, budgeting, and prayer, and include the gospel at every step. Mentorship turns doctrine into embodied help, showing young mothers they are not alone. Our culture is discipling women every day to believe that the plans God has for them will never bring joy. Teach them that true joy ONLY will come after obedience to God and His ways, which are always best.

title: What ethical issues arise with prenatal blood testing?
excerpt: Prenatal blood tests can be helpful for care but are often used to target children for abortion. The information gained must be stewarded for the child’s good, not destruction. Couples should reject any counsel that treats disability as a death…
content: Prenatal blood tests can be helpful for care but are often used to target children for abortion. The information gained must be stewarded for the child’s good, not destruction. Couples should reject any counsel that treats disability as a death sentence and seek physicians who honor life at every stage.

title: How can Christians keep the gospel central in abolition activism?
excerpt: Without the gospel, activism becomes anger or self righteousness. Keep Christ crucified and risen at the center: preach repentance and faith, confess your own sins, and extend mercy to all involved in abortion. Let activism flow from worship and love…
content: Without the gospel, activism becomes anger or self righteousness. Keep Christ crucified and risen at the center: preach repentance and faith, confess your own sins, and extend mercy to all involved in abortion. Let activism flow from worship and love for Christ, so your labor is in the Lord and not in the flesh.

title: What should Christians know about so-called abortion pill reversal?
excerpt: Some report that progesterone given after the first abortion pill can halt the process. Evidence is contested, but Christians should not promise what is uncertain. The surest righteousness is to refuse the first pill and rescue the child. Advocate for…
content: Some report that progesterone given after the first abortion pill can halt the process. Evidence is contested, but Christians should not promise what is uncertain. The surest righteousness is to refuse the first pill and rescue the child. Advocate for mothers in crisis, offer medical help that honors both lives, and speak truth without false hope.

title: How can churches build coalitions for abolition in their region?
excerpt: Start with prayer and shared doctrine: agree on the sin of abortion, and the authority of Christ. Gather pastors to repent of apathy, plan joint mercy efforts, and speak with one voice to magistrates. Cooperation around truth multiplies courage and…
content: Start with prayer and shared doctrine: agree on the sin of abortion, and the authority of Christ. Gather pastors to repent of apathy, plan joint mercy efforts, and speak with one voice to magistrates. Cooperation around truth multiplies courage and care for mothers and children.

title: What does Luke 1 teach about the unborn?
excerpt: Luke 1:41 shows unborn John the Baptist leaping for joy at unborn Jesus. This is real worship in the womb. The scripture treats these humans as more than just tissue, John the Baptist's soul is capable of the worship of…
content: Luke 1:41 shows unborn John the Baptist leaping for joy at unborn Jesus. This is real worship in the womb. The scripture treats these humans as more than just tissue, John the Baptist's soul is capable of the worship of God, and Jesus is known by his cousin as the person He Is, the Great I Am. The unborn are people whose lives must be protected. God is already at work in the smallest of humans!

title: How can Christians answer claims that criminalizing abortion harms women?
excerpt: True harm is done by abortion itself. Equal justice protects women by upholding their duty not to kill and by restraining those who prey on them. Laws should pair penalties with mercy provisions for those coerced, while still naming the…
content: True harm is done by abortion itself. Equal justice protects women by upholding their duty not to kill and by restraining those who prey on them. Laws should pair penalties with mercy provisions for those coerced, while still naming the act what it is. God’s law is for our good; sin destroys, but Christ heals. The Christian answer is rooted in a fundamental challenge to the premise of the question, establishing God’s authority over human experience. 1. Reaffirm the Non Negotiable Premise: The Value of Life The Christian must first hold fast to the non negotiable theological conviction: abortion is the unjust taking of a human life that was made by God. This is not a political position but an act of glorifying God by honoring the life He gives. As Scripture notes, "He himself gives to all mankind life and breath and everything else..." (Acts 17:25). The life in the womb is therefore a gift and a creation of God, and to kill that person, even whe the pregnancy is difficult, cannot be accepted as a moral good. 2. Uphold Divine Wisdom Over Human Logic When faced with claims that criminalizing abortion causes "harm" (which often prioritizes immediate earthly comfort or perceived risk), the Christian response is to place God's wisdom above human understanding. This stance requires unwavering faith: "Trust in the LORD with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding;" (Proverbs 3:5). An endeavor to "save" a woman from hardship that results in violating God’s clear command against taking innocent life is to idolize man's wisdom over a fear of God. 3. Conclude That Protecting Life Is the Opposite of Harm Therefore, the claim that criminalizing abortion harms women is answered by clarifying that the Christian purpose is to protect God's righteous standard. Christians must not concede to accepting blurred categories (such as presumptuous notions of risk) that would make the tragic destruction of a baby's life acceptable. The act of legislating to protect the life of the unborn is thus viewed as the opposite of harming women, because it promotes God's standard of justice and righteousness for all human life.

title: What hope does Revelation offer to persecuted abolitionists?
excerpt: Revelation shows the Lamb reigning and the martyrs vindicated. Those who resist the dragon’s war on children share in Christ’s sufferings but also in His victory. Persevering witnesses conquer by the blood of the Lamb and the word of their…
content: Revelation shows the Lamb reigning and the martyrs vindicated. Those who resist the dragon’s war on children share in Christ’s sufferings but also in His victory. Persevering witnesses conquer by the blood of the Lamb and the word of their testimony (Revelation 12:11). Endurance is worth it; Jesus will wipe every tear.

title: What scientific evidence supports protecting preborn children?
excerpt: Modern embryology recognizes that human life begins at fertilization, when a distinct DNA code is formed. Ultrasound imagery, fetal heartbeat detection, and pain response studies all reveal an active, developing child rather than impersonal tissue. Science therefore confirms what Scripture…
content: Modern embryology recognizes that human life begins at fertilization, when a distinct DNA code is formed. Ultrasound imagery, fetal heartbeat detection, and pain response studies all reveal an active, developing child rather than impersonal tissue. Science therefore confirms what Scripture declares—that every preborn child is a human neighbor worthy of protection.

title: Does the Bible affirm the personhood of the unborn?
excerpt: Scripture treats the unborn as living persons known by God. Psalm 139, Jeremiah 1, and Luke 1 all testify that the Lord fashions, calls, and even fills children with the Holy Spirit (John the Baptist) while they are yet in…
content: Scripture treats the unborn as living persons known by God. Psalm 139, Jeremiah 1, and Luke 1 all testify that the Lord fashions, calls, and even fills children with the Holy Spirit (John the Baptist) while they are yet in the womb. Because God creates and values preborn life, the church must speak and act to defend it. Exodus 21:22 24 makes it clear that there is a distinction for when a pregnant woman loses her child even accidentally. The man causing the death of her child is required to pay "life for life." How much more serious is it if someone intentionally murders the unborn?

title: How should churches walk with repentant post-abortive parents?
excerpt: Churches must lead with the gospel, assuring repentant parents that Christ’s blood truly cleanses every sin. Pair that hope with patient discipleship, trauma informed counseling, and intergenerational fellowship that refuses to stigmatize the broken. As the body bears their burdens,…
content: Churches must lead with the gospel, assuring repentant parents that Christ’s blood truly cleanses every sin. Pair that hope with patient discipleship, trauma informed counseling, and intergenerational fellowship that refuses to stigmatize the broken. As the body bears their burdens, the repentant can heal, testify, and join the mission to protect other families.

title: How should we care for mothers?
excerpt: We begin by reminding them that the God who sees Hagar also sees them. We speak plainly about the value of their child while offering tangible help, local church support, and gospel hope.
content: We begin by reminding them that the God who sees Hagar also sees them. We speak plainly about the value of their child while offering tangible help, local church support, and gospel hope. Extended Explanation: Caring for Mothers A robust abolitionist response includes: Presence : Offer prayer, discipleship, and accountability through a local church body. Provision : Connect families with housing, medical care, and employment resources. Protection : Advocate for laws that defend both mother and child. Proclamation : Share the gospel that calls all of us to repentance and offers rest in Christ. Matthew 28:19 When we walk with mothers, meeting needs while declaring truth, we display the justice and mercy of Christ.

title: What practical steps can communities take to stand with mothers?
excerpt: Communities can build coalitions of churches, crisis pregnancy workers, medical professionals, and mentors who respond the moment a mother asks for help. Provide housing, childcare, legal aid, and job pathways so she never feels cornered by fear. When neighbors show…
content: Communities can build coalitions of churches, crisis pregnancy workers, medical professionals, and mentors who respond the moment a mother asks for help. Provide housing, childcare, legal aid, and job pathways so she never feels cornered by fear. When neighbors show up with tangible love, abortion becomes unthinkable and families thrive.

title: Is it compassionate to prosecute women for having abortions?
excerpt: Yes, and we encourage you to study and understand the longer explanation given. 1. It is compassionate toward the children who are being aborted . 2. It is compassionate toward the women and men who are tempted to murder their…
content: Yes, and we encourage you to study and understand the longer explanation given. 1. It is compassionate toward the children who are being aborted . 2. It is compassionate toward the women and men who are tempted to murder their preborn child. 3. It is compassionate toward women who would earn this punishment by continuing to kill their child. 4. It is compassionate toward the whole of our country, being that we are a blood guilty nation under the judgement of God. Yes. However, in order understand this, we must understand the nature of that goal, and the foundation of it. We must begin, therefore, with what God has said. Exodus 20:13 clearly states, "You shall not murder." God Himself had already given the punishment for murder, namely, the death penalty, in Genesis 9:6, and Romans 13:3 4 makes especially clear that this is not only appropriate, but the undeniable responsibility of the governing authorities. The answer to the question above is illuminated, when we begin with the foundational question: "Is God, who instituted the death penalty for all murderers, a compassionate God?" The answer to this question is most certainly that He is, and the whole of His law, therefore, is in keeping with His character. That said, there are four more ways in which we, even by human reasoning, based in Scripture, can see how it is that it will be compassionate to prosecute women for having abortions. First, it is compassionate toward the children who are being aborted . Simply put, protecting the innocent and enacting justice on their behalf is always compassionate. This ought not be controversial, but we live in a country that disregards children altogether and cares nothing for their well being. Indeed, those who would not protect children from being killed in the womb are the ones who have no compassion (Proverbs 12:10). Second, it is compassionate toward the women who are tempted to murder their preborn child. The accusation that we who believe in abolition receive is that we actively desire to see women punished. To the contrary, we desire to see women stop earning earthly consequences for that choice, and more importantly, the punishment of God (Revelation 21:8). We desire to abolish and criminalize abortion not so that we can punish the women, but so that they will stop having abortions . This is the only compassionate outcome that we can desire for them, because while abortion is harmful physically for the children, it is eternally (and even temporarily) harmful for the women murdering them. We desire, then, that those women who might otherwise destroy themselves by making themselves murderers, would not do so, and would not in so doing earn for themselves greater judgment from God and man. This would certainly be the inevitable effect of abolition. On this note, for the sake of clarification, this applies to men and women. The accusation that we receive is that we are focused solely upon punishing women, but our desire is not to criminalize women, but to criminalize the act of abortion, and in turn, to prosecute all those that murder or participate in murder in that way, including and especially murderous fathers. Third, it is compassionate toward women who would still abort their children, even with the knowledge of the punishment. This is because ultimately, abortion is not against the child, but against God (Psalm 51:4), and the women who murder their children need to be confronted with that reality. Facing the punishment due to a murderer is an opportunity for these women to recognize who they are sinners in need of a savior and it's an opportunity for us to proclaim to them again the gospel of forgiveness, which only makes sense to someone who has seen they have done wrong. Fourth, it is compassionate toward the whole of our country, being that we are a blood guilty nation under the judgement of God. We desire to repent as a nation (Psalm 106:6,Psalm 106:38), that we not perish for our wickedness. It will not be well with us if we continue to commit this most horrific genocide.

title: Is miscarriage the same as abortion?
excerpt: No. Miscarriage is the tragic death of a child in the womb by natural causes. Abortion is the intentional killing of a child in the womb. The deaths may both be grievous, but they are not morally the same because…
content: No. Miscarriage is the tragic death of a child in the womb by natural causes. Abortion is the intentional killing of a child in the womb. The deaths may both be grievous, but they are not morally the same because the cause and intent are not the same. Scripture consistently treats the shedding of innocent blood as a matter of guilt before God (Exodus 20:13; Proverbs 6:16 17). That means intention matters. When a mother suffers miscarriage, she has not become a murderer. She should be met with compassion, mourning, prayer, and practical care, not suspicion or accusation (Romans 12:15). The abolitionist position opposes the deliberate killing of preborn children, not the natural sorrow of losing a child. Christians should speak carefully here, because confused language can wound grieving parents and blur the real evil of abortion.

title: Will abortion laws criminalize a mother who has had a miscarriage?
excerpt: Absolutely NOT. A miscarriage is not the same act as an abortion, and just laws will not treat them as the same thing. Equal justice requires punishing the intentional shedding of innocent blood, not treating every tragic pregnancy loss as…
content: Absolutely NOT. A miscarriage is not the same act as an abortion, and just laws will not treat them as the same thing. Equal justice requires punishing the intentional shedding of innocent blood, not treating every tragic pregnancy loss as a crime (Romans 13:3 4). Laws against abortion should target intentional acts of killing, along with those who aid, counsel, or profit from them. They should require evidence, due process, and careful distinctions between natural miscarriage, premature delivery, medical emergencies, and deliberate abortion. That is basic justice, and abolitionists should be clear about it. At the same time, a law that never allows investigation of any suspicious death would fail to protect preborn children. The goal is not to harass grieving mothers, but to uphold truth with righteous judgment (Zechariah 7:9). Pastors, families, and magistrates should therefore reject fearmongering while insisting on both compassion for sufferers and justice for victims. Our legal system already has provisions and methods for law enforcement to decide when a death requires investigation. The same standards need to apply to the death of the preborn.
